title: Conjecture 1.1 (Guo, Kang and Zwaneveld)
statement_text: For every graph $G$ and every integer $d\geq 0$, we have $\chi(G)=\chi^{d}(G\boxtimes K_{d+1})$.
context_text: Motivated by studying the tightness of Hoffman's spectral bound on the chromatic number and its $d$-defective extension by Bilu, Guo, Kang and Zwaneveld conjectured an exact equality between the ordinary chromatic number of $G$ and the $d$-defective chromatic number of its $(d+1)$-fold clique blowup. The conjecture was also posed as an open problem at the 2025 Graph Theory workshop in Oberwolfach. The easy direction $\chi^{d}(G\boxtimes K_{d+1})\leq\chi(G)$ holds by lifting an optimal proper coloring; the heart of the conjecture is the reverse inequality.
paper_contribution: Disproves the conjecture by constructing graphs $G$ of arbitrarily large chromatic number such that $\chi(G)\geq\frac{30}{29}\chi^{d}(G\boxtimes K_{d+1})$ for infinitely many $d$; on the positive side proves the constant-factor version $\chi^{d}(G\boxtimes K_{d+1})\leq\chi(G)\leq 2\chi^{d}(G\boxtimes K_{d+1})$ for every graph $G$ and $d\geq 0$.

title: Problem 1.4
statement_text: Determine the smallest value of $c$ for which the inequality $\chi(G)\leq c\cdot\chi^{d}(G\boxtimes K_{d+1})$ always holds.
context_text: Theorem 1.2 (for $d=2$) provides a graph showing that $c\geq\frac{30}{29}$ is necessary, while Theorem 1.3 gives the upper bound $c\leq 2$. A bootstrapped version of Theorem 1.2 further shows that assuming $\chi(G)$ or $d$ to be large cannot reduce the constant factor arbitrarily close to $1$.
notes: The theorem environment statement is truncated to 'Determine' in the source; the full problem is reconstructed from the immediately preceding context.
